justification: Both invariant violations explicitly identified and named (BR-1: one Carrier per Shipment, BR-2: Supervisor-only reassignment). Two detailed escalations produced with specific reference to the violated invariants. No acceptance criteria written (acceptance_criteria array is empty). Options proposed under each escalation are clearly labeled as proposals with recommendations. Confidence level is 45, well within the ≤55 threshold.

observations.text: Both invariant violations (1:1 Carrier and Supervisor-only reassignment) are explicitly identified, named, and given formal business rule IDs (BR-1, BR-2)
Acceptance criteria array is intentionally empty with clear reasoning that no valid criteria can be defined until contradictions are resolved
Escalations are formal, structured, and reference specific business rules by ID
Each escalation provides multiple options clearly labeled as proposals, not decisions — avoids the failure mode of presenting reconciliation as accepted requirement
Confidence level of 45 with well-reasoned justification for low confidence
Edge cases are excellent — identifies parent shipment lifecycle gap, accountability fracture, customer consent issue, and boundary instability
Added a third invariant (BR-3: dispatch-gate) that strengthens the analysis beyond what was expected
Business impact analysis is thorough, covering financial, operational, user, legal/compliance dimensions with specific stakeholder groups
Rejection reasons section provides a clean summary mapping each violation to the broken business rule
